design_decisions.x86_lapic_mapping: Hard-coded LAPIC. QEMU microvm has NO 8259 PIC, NO 8253/PIT, NO IOAPIC (RTC off), so the periodic source MUST be the LAPIC timer; under tb-vmm the in-kernel KVM LAPIC (KVM_CREATE_IRQCHIP) gives the same model. CORRECTED RATIONALE (both reviewers flagged the candidates' false 'inside a WB 2 MiB super-page' premise): the LAPIC register page PA 0xFEE0_0000 sits in the 4th GiB, ABOVE the boot identity map [0,1 GiB) (x86_64 boot.rs maps exactly 512x2 MiB pages), so that PA is simply UNMAPPED -- there is NO WB alias to resolve and NO super-page to split. The fresh UC page is the SOLE LAPIC mapping. A new mmu primitive map_device_page(va,pa) (mirrors map_heap_frames, reusing ensure_table/level_index/make_entry/invlpg + M6 frames) splices a 4 KiB page in the vacant PML4[3] device window (LAPIC_WINDOW_VA=0x0000_0180_0000_0000; PML4[0]=boot, [1]=M4 user, [2]=M7 heap, [3]=device) onto 0xFEE0_0000 with leaf P|RW|PWT|PCD|NX. PWT|PCD with the PTE PAT bit clear selects PAT entry 3 = strong UC under the power-up IA32_PAT default mmu_init kept; NX is grafted from the correctness candidate (EFER.NXE already on since M3 -- an MMIO register page is never fetched). timer.rs reaches every LAPIC register as a volatile u32 through LAPIC_WINDOW_VA+offset.
design_decisions.x86_irq_dispatch: NO idt.rs change: idt::init already installed all 256 gates as DPL0 64-bit INTERRUPT gates -> __trap_thunk_N -> __alltraps (saves+restores all 15 GPRs) -> x86_trap_handler, so vector 0x20 already lands there with IF cleared by the gate (no nesting). x86_trap_handler gains ONE early branch right after reading f.vector: `if super::timer::try_handle_irq(vector) { return; }`. NARROW + fail-closed recognition (grafted from the correctness candidate over the base's blanket 'vector>=32 -> EOI'): only 0x20 (timer -> crate::dispatch_irq, then LAPIC EOI write 0->0xB0, return true) and 0xFF (spurious -> return true, NO EOI, SDM Vol.3A 11.9) are ours; any other vector returns false and falls through to the existing synchronous classifier (Other -> Halt), so a stray external vector a future milestone routes through the thunks is not silently swallowed. On true, __alltraps restores the FULL frame and iretq's back to the exact interrupted instruction. M4's int 0x80 bypasses this handler via its dedicated DPL3 gate, so it is unaffected.
design_decisions.x86_timer: LAPIC timer, periodic. lapic_enable(): rdmsr/wrmsr OR IA32_APIC_BASE.EN (bit 11, base preserved) -- grafted from the correctness candidate so a LAPIC left hardware-disabled by any boot path still delivers ticks -- then TPR=0 (accept all priorities) and SVR(0xF0)=bit8|0xFF (software enable + spurious vector 0xFF). lapic_timer_arm(): Divide(0x3E0)=0b1011 (divide-by-1), LVT Timer(0x320)=0x20 | periodic(bit17), Initial Count(0x380)=0x0010_0000 (uncalibrated by design; ~1 ms/tick under both KVM and TCG -- 16 ticks ~16 ms, far inside the 15 s runner timeout). irq_enable() issues the kernel's FIRST sti, only inside the canary window; after >=16 ticks irq_disable() (cli) then lapic_timer_disarm() (LVT mask bit16 + Initial Count=0) so no IRQ survives into halt. read_cycle_counter()=rdtsc (EDX:EAX). timer.rs is write-only on the MMIO path (no read_volatile import, no dead lapic_read) so the x86 HAL stays warning-clean.
design_decisions.aarch64_gic: GICv2, hard-coded (QEMU virt -cpu cortex-a72, no gic-version override => v2; v3's ICC_* sysreg path is a deferred follow-up the run scripts pin against). GICD @ 0x0800_0000, GICC @ 0x0801_0000, both already inside the L1[0] Device-nGnRnE identity gigabyte mmu_init mapped -- NO new MMIO mapping. gic_init(): GICD_CTLR(0x000)=bit0 (EnableGrp0), GICD_ISENABLER0(0x100)=1<<30 (enable timer PPI 30), GICC_PMR(0x004)=0xFF (allow all priorities), GICC_CTLR(0x000)=bit0 (enable CPU iface), then dsb sy;isb. Ack=read GICC_IAR(0x00C) (INTID=bits[9:0]); EOI=write the FULL IAR value to GICC_EOIR(0x010). Spurious guard intid>=1020 (covers all special INTIDs 1020-1023; kept from the robustness base over the rejected candidate's narrow ==1023) -> no tick, no EOI; any other non-spurious INTID is still EOIed (correct GICv2 hygiene).
design_decisions.aarch64_timer: EL1 non-secure physical timer (CNTP_*), PPI INTID 30. timer_start(): CNTP_TVAL_EL0 = (CNTFRQ_EL0/1000).max(0x1000) (~1 ms @ the virt 62.5 MHz CNTFRQ, floored against an implausible frequency), CNTP_CTL_EL0=1 (ENABLE, IMASK=0), isb. TVAL is one-shot, so handle_irq() reloads CNTP_TVAL_EL0 BEFORE EOI -- which also DE-ASSERTS the level-sensitive PPI (clears CNTP_CTL.ISTATUS) so it does not immediately re-fire after EOI -- making it periodic. irq_unmask() issues the kernel's FIRST 'msr daifclr,#2'; after the canary irq_mask() ('msr daifset,#2') + timer_stop() (CNTP_CTL_EL0=0) re-mask before the marker/halt. The Current-EL-SPx IRQ slot at vectors.rs offset 0x280 (was __vec_other) becomes the real __vec_irq: SAVE_CONTEXT; mov x1,#2; b __trap_dispatch -- reuses the existing 0x110-byte frame + RESTORE_CONTEXT/eret. aarch64_trap_handler branches on source==2 BEFORE dereferencing the raw frame, calls timer::handle_irq(), and returns WITHOUT touching ELR_EL1 (an async IRQ resumes AT the interrupted instruction, unlike the synchronous brk +4). read_cycle_counter()=CNTPCT_EL0 (isb-fenced).
design_decisions.irq_hook_mechanism: A dedicated arch-neutral SAFE seam in lib.rs, SEPARATE from the synchronous TRAP_HOOK: static TIMER_TICKS: AtomicU64 + static IRQ_HOOK: AtomicUsize, pub fn tick_count(), pub fn set_irq_hook(fn(u64)), pub(crate) fn dispatch_irq(irq_id) { TIMER_TICKS.fetch_add(1, Relaxed); then run the registered fn(u64) hook if non-zero }. SINGLE source of truth for the tick (grafted from the correctness candidate over the base's per-arch TICKS statics, per all three reviewers' cross-graft): each per-arch handler calls crate::dispatch_irq ONLY for its timer vector/PPI, so the bump lives in exactly one place and matches the stated M9 seam 'dispatch_irq bumps the tick then runs the hook'. Chosen over extending TrapKind::Irq through TRAP_HOOK because a timer IRQ carries none of TrapInfo's synchronous-fault semantics (fault_addr/cause/Resume-vs-Halt) and the kernel's trap policy halts on anything but #BP; M9 just calls set_irq_hook(schedule) with zero rework. For M8 the hook is left unset -- dispatch_irq only counts the tick, which the register-integrity canary reads via crate::tick_count() (an atomic load the optimiser cannot hoist out of the spin).
design_decisions.in_guest_timing: tb_hal::read_cycle_counter() = rdtsc (x86_64) / CNTPCT_EL0 (aarch64, isb-fenced) -- a monotonic, VMM-independent clock. The unsafe-free kernel samples boot_c0 as the FIRST statement of rust_main (a pure register read needing no init) and boot_c1 immediately after the M8 marker, then prints one line 'boot-cycles=0x<delta>' via the local write_hex_u64, right before halt -- the honest guest-only boot figure (vCPU-first-instruction -> M0..M8 self-test done) docs/BENCHMARKS.md said would land at M8. Per BENCHMARKS sec.5 the standing quotable figure is this counter read from a --release build under tb-vmm. Note the implementation reads the PHYSICAL counter CNTPCT_EL0, not the doc's earlier CNTVCT_EL0 -- BENCHMARKS is corrected to match the code.

use core::sync::atomic::{AtomicBool, AtomicUsize, Ordering};
patches.new_string:

patches.old_string:
pub mod pmm;

pub use serial::{serial_init, serial_write_byte};

pub use pmm::pmm_collect_regions;

use core::sync::atomic::{AtomicBool, Ordering};

mod trap; // Rust trap dispatch + breakpoint(); the only raw-frame deref.
patches.new_string:
mod serial; // PL011 @ 0x0900_0000 (QEMU `virt` UART0).
mod timer; // M8: GICv2 + EL1 physical timer (PPI 30); IRQ ack/EOI/tick + CNTPCT.
mod trap; // Rust trap dispatch + breakpoint(); the only raw-frame deref.

patches.old_string:
pub use serial::{serial_init, serial_write_byte};
pub use trap::breakpoint;

/// kernel-heap leaves set it — the heap holds data, never executable code.
const PTE_NX: u64 = 1 << 63;

// Source tag passed in x1 by the `vectors.rs` trampolines.
const SOURCE_SYNC_CURRENT_SPX: u64 = 0; // real handler (synchronous, our EL)
VEC_SLOT __vec_other          // 0x280 IRQ / vIRQ   (masked in M1)
patches.new_string: VEC_SLOT __vec_irq            // 0x280 IRQ / vIRQ   <-- real handler (M8)

patches.old_string:
__vec_other:                      // every other vector: unexpected -> fatal
    SAVE_CONTEXT
    mov  x1, #1                   // source = 1: unexpected vector
    b    __trap_dispatch
patches.new_string:
__vec_other:                      // every other vector: unexpected -> fatal
    SAVE_CONTEXT
    mov  x1, #1                   // source = 1: unexpected vector
    b    __trap_dispatch
__vec_irq:                        // Current-EL-SPx IRQ: the M8 periodic timer
    SAVE_CONTEXT
    mov  x1, #2                   // source = 2: asynchronous IRQ (current EL)
    b    __trap_dispatch

patches.old_string:
# scripts/run-x86_64.sh - QEMU launcher + Definition-of-Done check for M7 (x86_64).
#
# Boots the PVH ELF kernel under QEMU 'microvm' (the machine type Firecracker is
# modelled on), wires legacy 16550 COM1 to stdio, and asserts the EXACT marker
# "M7: heap OK" appears on serial (the newest cumulative-boot milestone; the
# kernel grows its frame-backed heap past the fixed 2 MiB arena and proves it).
# M0's hello, M1's trap round-trip, M2's ping-pong, M3's "M3: mmu OK", M4's
# "M4: user/ring OK", M5's "M5: alloc OK" and M6's "M6: frame alloc OK" all
# print earlier in the same boot, so one run proves every milestone. Fail-closed:
patches.new_string:
# scripts/run-x86_64.sh - QEMU launcher + Definition-of-Done check for M8 (x86_64).
#
# Boots the PVH ELF kernel under QEMU 'microvm' (the machine type Firecracker is
# modelled on), wires legacy 16550 COM1 to stdio, and asserts the EXACT marker
# "M8: timer OK" appears on serial (the newest cumulative-boot milestone; the
# kernel brings up the LAPIC + LAPIC timer and takes its first async interrupt).
# M0's hello, M1's trap round-trip, M2's ping-pong, "M3: mmu OK",
# "M4: user/ring OK", "M5: alloc OK", "M6: frame alloc OK" and "M7: heap OK" all
# print earlier in the same boot, so one run proves every milestone. Fail-closed:

patches.old_string: MARKER='M7: heap OK'
patches.new_string: MARKER='M8: timer OK'

patches.old_string:
# scripts/run-aarch64.sh -- QEMU `virt` runner (milestone M7: frame-backed heap).
#
# Boots the aarch64 tb-os image under QEMU, captures the PL011 serial stream,
# and asserts the executable Definition-of-Done marker "M7: heap OK" (the
# kernel grows its frame-backed heap past the fixed 2 MiB arena and proves it).
# M0's hello, M1's trap round-trip, M2's ping-pong, "M3: mmu OK",
# "M4: user/ring OK", "M5: alloc OK" and "M6: frame alloc OK" all print earlier
# in the same boot.
patches.new_string:
# scripts/run-aarch64.sh -- QEMU `virt` runner (milestone M8: async timer tick).
#
# Boots the aarch64 tb-os image under QEMU, captures the PL011 serial stream,
# and asserts the executable Definition-of-Done marker "M8: timer OK" (the
# kernel brings up GICv2 + the EL1 physical timer and takes its first async IRQ).
# M0's hello, M1's trap round-trip, M2's ping-pong, "M3: mmu OK",
# "M4: user/ring OK", "M5: alloc OK", "M6: frame alloc OK" and "M7: heap OK" all
# print earlier in the same boot.

patches.old_string: MARKER="M7: heap OK"
patches.new_string: MARKER="M8: timer OK"

patches.old_string:
# Boots the SAME kernel ELF the QEMU/PVH path boots, but through tb-vmm + the
# tb-boot v0 contract (direct 64-bit long mode, NO PVH note, NO A0 trampoline),
# and asserts the exact marker "M7: heap OK" on the guest serial console (M0-M6,
# including "M6: frame alloc OK", all print earlier in the same boot).
# Requires a usable /dev/kvm; if absent it SKIPS (exit 0) with a clear message.
patches.new_string:
# Boots the SAME kernel ELF the QEMU/PVH path boots, but through tb-vmm + the
# tb-boot v0 contract (direct 64-bit long mode, NO PVH note, NO A0 trampoline),
# and asserts the exact marker "M8: timer OK" on the guest serial console (M0-M7,
# including "M7: heap OK", all print earlier in the same boot). M8 makes tb-vmm
# create an in-kernel interrupt controller (KVM_CREATE_IRQCHIP) so the guest's
# LAPIC timer fires; with an in-kernel LAPIC the kernel's terminal `cli; hlt`
# parks IN-kernel (no KVM_EXIT_HLT), so tb-vmm runs to the wall-clock timeout
# while the serial device has already flushed every marker byte to the captured
# OUTPUT -- the grep over OUTPUT is the verdict, exactly like the QEMU runners.
# Requires a usable /dev/kvm; if absent it SKIPS (exit 0) with a clear message.

patches.old_string: MARKER='M7: heap OK'
patches.new_string: MARKER='M8: timer OK'

patches.old_string: #   * t_full   = spawn -> the final cumulative milestone marker ("M7: heap OK").
patches.new_string: #   * t_full   = spawn -> the final cumulative milestone marker ("M8: timer OK").

patches.old_string: FINAL_MARKER="M7: heap OK"
patches.new_string: FINAL_MARKER="M8: timer OK"

patches.old_string:
coproc QPROC { exec "${argv[@]}" 2>&1; }
  local qpid="${QPROC_PID}"
  ( sleep "${BENCH_TIMEOUT}"; kill "${qpid}" 2>/dev/null ) & local watch=$!
  local line ts
  while IFS= read -r line <&"${QPROC[0]}"; do
    ts="$(date +%s.%N)"
    [[ -z "${first_ts}" && "${line}" == *"${FIRST_MARKER}"* ]] && first_ts="${ts}"
    if [[ "${line}" == *"${FINAL_MARKER}"* ]]; then full_ts="${ts}"; break; fi
  done
  kill "${qpid}" 2>/dev/null || true
  kill "${watch}" 2>/dev/null || true
  wait "${qpid}" 2>/dev/null || true
patches.new_string:
# Capture serial to a temp LOG FILE and poll it with `grep` — do NOT drain a
  # live `coproc` read loop. The old loop ran a `$(date)` command substitution
  # per line; bash closes a coprocess's fds inside that subshell, so under KVM —
  # where the whole M0..M7 self-test streams out in a single burst — every line
  # after the first was dropped and the FINAL marker was never matched (full=NA
  # every iteration, while first still landed). Polling a regular file keeps
  # QEMU's real PID for the watchdog, and a post-run grep drains any marker
  # flushed in the last write before exit/kill.
  local log; log="$(mktemp)"
  "${argv[@]}" >"${log}" 2>&1 & local qpid=$!
  ( sleep "${BENCH_TIMEOUT}"; kill "${qpid}" 2>/dev/null ) & local watch=$!
  while kill -0 "${qpid}" 2>/dev/null; do
    [[ -z "${first_ts}" ]] && grep -qF -- "${FIRST_MARKER}" "${log}" && first_ts="$(date +%s.%N)"
    if grep -qF -- "${FINAL_MARKER}" "${log}"; then full_ts="$(date +%s.%N)"; break; fi
    sleep 0.002
  done
  # Final drain: a marker in the last buffered write still counts (timed now).
  [[ -z "${first_ts}" ]] && grep -qF -- "${FIRST_MARKER}" "${log}" 2>/dev/null && first_ts="$(date +%s.%N)"
  [[ -z "${full_ts}" ]] && grep -qF -- "${FINAL_MARKER}" "${log}" 2>/dev/null && full_ts="$(date +%s.%N)"
  kill "${qpid}" 2>/dev/null || true
  kill "${watch}" 2>/dev/null || true
  wait "${qpid}" 2>/dev/null || true
  rm -f "${log}"

patches.old_string:
- **boot+selftest** = spawn → the final cumulative marker (currently
  `M7: heap OK`). This is boot **plus the entire M0…latest self-test** (M2's
  1000-round ping-pong, M6's ~65 k free-frame `seed()`, and M7's three 4 MiB
  page-table-mapped + pattern-verified heap growths), so it is labelled
  separately and is **not** a pure boot number.
patches.new_string:
- **boot+selftest** = spawn → the final cumulative marker (currently
  `M8: timer OK`). This is boot **plus the entire M0…latest self-test** (M2's
  1000-round ping-pong, M6's ~65 k free-frame `seed()`, M7's three 4 MiB
  page-table-mapped + pattern-verified heap growths, and M8's
  first-async-interrupt register-integrity canary), so it is labelled separately
  and is **not** a pure boot number.

patches.old_string: ### TABOS measured — today (M7, kernel boots a self-test then halts)
patches.new_string: ### TABOS measured — today (M8, kernel boots a self-test then halts)

patches.old_string: > **boot-to-first-output** is stable across M5→M7 (~28 ms TCG — just VMM-spawn +
patches.new_string: > **boot-to-first-output** is stable across M5→M8 (~28 ms TCG — just VMM-spawn +

patches.old_string:
> So a clean, VMM-independent **guest-only** boot figure (the one that places
> TABOS next to Unikraft/OSv in Bucket 1) requires **in-guest cycle timing**
> (`rdtsc` / `CNTVCT`+`CNTFRQ`) — which lands at **M8** (the timer milestone),
> where a second clock (guest-first-instruction → ready) is added. Until then,
> the honest, defensible TABOS claim is **architectural** (§4): the firmware +
> bootloader + decompress + Linux-kernel-init budget — tens to hundreds of ms in
> Bucket 2 — that a from-scratch PVH/`tb-boot` kernel **never executes at all**.
patches.new_string:
> A clean, VMM-independent **guest-only** boot figure (the one that places TABOS
> next to Unikraft/OSv in Bucket 1) needs **in-guest cycle timing** — which
> **landed at M8**: `tb_hal::read_cycle_counter()` reads `rdtsc` (x86_64) /
> `CNTPCT_EL0` (aarch64), the kernel samples it at `rust_main` entry and just
> after the `M8: timer OK` marker, and prints the guest-only delta as a
> `boot-cycles=0x…` serial line — a monotonic span (vCPU-first-instruction →
> M0..M8 self-test done) independent of the VMM/host floor. The next refinement
> is a true guest-first-instruction → agent-ready second clock once an
> agent-ready state exists. The standing architectural claim (§4) is unchanged:
> the firmware + bootloader + decompress + Linux-kernel-init budget — tens to
> hundreds of ms in Bucket 2 — that a from-scratch PVH/`tb-boot` kernel **never
> executes at all**.

patches.old_string:
scheduling noise. The defensible number is **`rdtsc` (x86_64) / `CNTVCT_EL0`
  + `CNTFRQ_EL0` (aarch64) read in-guest, release build, under `tb-vmm`** —
  landing with the M8 timer milestone. Until then, `boot-to-first-output` under
  QEMU/KVM is reported only as a coarse, VMM-floor-dominated sanity figure, not
  a competitor comparison.
patches.new_string:
scheduling noise. The defensible number is **`rdtsc` (x86_64) / `CNTPCT_EL0`
  (aarch64) read in-guest, release build, under `tb-vmm`** — which **landed at
  M8**: `tb_hal::read_cycle_counter()` is sampled at `rust_main` entry and just
  after the `M8: timer OK` marker, and the kernel prints the guest-only
  `boot-cycles=0x…` delta. `boot-to-first-output` under QEMU/KVM remains only a
  coarse, VMM-floor-dominated sanity figure, not a competitor comparison.

patches.old_string:
let vm = kvm.create_vm()?;

        // Guest RAM + KVM memslots.

selftest: Build BOTH targets from the repo root (MINGW64): `cargo kbuild --target targets/x86_64-tabos-none.json` and `cargo kbuild --target targets/aarch64-tabos-none.json` — both must compile with 0 warnings (the kernel crate gains ZERO unsafe{} blocks; ALL new unsafe/asm is in tb-hal: the two new timer.rs, the one-line trap.rs intercepts, mmu::map_device_page, the vectors.rs __vec_irq trampoline, and the lib.rs dispatch_irq transmute). Then boot-assert all three runners: `bash scripts/run-x86_64.sh` (QEMU microvm), `bash scripts/run-aarch64.sh` (QEMU virt), `bash scripts/run-vmm-x86_64.sh` (tb-vmm + KVM). Each greps for the new exact marker `M8: timer OK`, which prints AFTER `M7: heap OK`. Expected serial tail every boot: `...M7: heap OK` / `timer-test: taking the first async interrupt` / `M8: timer OK` / `boot-cycles=0x<delta>` then halt. Cumulative M0..M7 markers (hello, M1 traps, M2 context-switch, M3 mmu, M4 user/ring, M5 alloc, M6 frame alloc, M7 heap) all still print earlier in the SAME boot, proving no regression. tb-vmm note: with the in-kernel LAPIC the guest's terminal cli;hlt parks in-kernel (no KVM_EXIT_HLT), so run-vmm-x86_64.sh runs to its ~30 s wall-clock guard while the per-byte-flushed marker is already in the captured OUTPUT — the grep is the verdict (exit code is irrelevant, `set +e` around the capture). The dead-timer path is fail-closed: the canary bails at CANARY_CAP (100M iters, ~tens of ms at KVM speed) then `tick_count() >= 16` is false → `M8: FAIL ...` prints and the runner reports the missing marker, never tripping the timeout silently.
bench: `ITER=20 bash scripts/bench-boot.sh x86_64` (and aarch64) now stamps boot+selftest on the new FINAL_MARKER `M8: timer OK`. The FLAKE-2 fix replaces the coproc live-read with a temp-file capture + `grep -qF` poll + a post-run drain grep, so under KVM (where the whole M0..M8 self-test streams out in one burst) the final marker is reliably matched instead of full=NA every iteration; boot-to-first-output timing (spawn → `hello from rust_main`) is preserved. SEPARATELY, the new in-guest metric: every boot the kernel prints `boot-cycles=0x<delta>` = read_cycle_counter() sampled at rust_main entry vs just after the M8 marker (rdtsc on x86_64 / CNTPCT_EL0 on aarch64) — the honest, VMM-independent guest-only span. Per BENCHMARKS §5 the quotable figure is this counter from a `--release` build under tb-vmm; it is still boot+self-test (dominated by M2 ping-pong + M6/M7 frame churn), not pure boot, until an agent-ready endpoint supplies a second clock. No fabricated number is added to the measured table — only the metric mechanism and the standing-rule prose are updated.
build_notes: Watch items when compiling: (1) 0-WARNING gate — the two timer.rs are deliberately warning-clean: x86 timer.rs is WRITE-ONLY on the MMIO path (no `read_volatile` import, NO dead `lapic_read` — the rejected candidate's dead fn + orphaned import are intentionally NOT carried); neither timer.rs imports `AtomicU64`/`Ordering` because the tick counter is centralized in lib.rs, so no unused-import warning; every private const/fn in both files is referenced (verified). (2) lib.rs needs `AtomicU64` ADDED to the `core::sync::atomic` import (done); `TIMER_TICKS`/`IRQ_HOOK` are private statics (no missing_docs) and `dispatch_irq` is pub(crate) (no missing_docs required) — the four NEW pub fns (tick_count/set_irq_hook/timer_demo/read_cycle_counter) all carry doc comments for `#![deny(missing_docs)]`. (3) Edition is 2021 for tb-hal and kernel, so `unsafe_op_in_unsafe_fn` is allow-by-default — `map_one_device_page` (an `unsafe fn`) calls ensure_table/write_volatile/invlpg WITHOUT inner unsafe blocks, exactly like the existing `map_one_heap_page`, so it stays clean. (4) NO idt.rs change — vector 0x20 is already a present DPL0 interrupt gate from idt::init's 256-gate table; if a future change ever made the IDT sparse this would need revisiting. (5) Timer counts: LAPIC initial 0x10_0000 + divide-by-1 ≈ 1 ms/tick; CNTP TVAL = CNTFRQ/1000 ≈ 1 ms; 16 ticks ≈ 16 ms under both KVM and TCG (TCG's APIC/CNTP timers run on the host-time virtual clock, so ticks accrue even while the emulated canary runs slowly) — well inside the 15 s runner timeout. (6) tb-vmm: `VmFd::create_irq_chip()` exists in kvm-ioctls 0.24 (the pinned version) and `From<kvm_ioctls::Error> for VmmError` exists (error.rs:123), so `vm.create_irq_chip()?` compiles; it is placed AFTER create_vm and BEFORE create_vcpu (required ordering). The MINIMAL tb-vmm path was chosen (only create_irq_chip): the truncated MarkerSink/--success-marker chain is NOT added (avoids cli.rs/main.rs churn and 0-warning risk in tb-vmm), because serial.rs `transmit` flushes stdout per byte and the run script greps captured OUTPUT under `timeout --foreground` regardless of exit code. No KVM_SET_IDENTITY_MAP_ADDR is added — the guest enters in long mode and never uses the real-mode emulation path, and KVM supplies a default identity-map page; the existing late `set_tss_address(0xfffbd000)` in arch::setup runs before the first KVM_RUN and suffices. (7) Rust-source-string escapes: the kernel/lib patches contain `\\n` Rust string escapes and quoted markers — ensure no trailing-whitespace drift on apply. (8) aarch64 `asm!` uses `{v}` named operands and multi-template forms (`asm!("isb", "mrs {v}, cntpct_el0", ...)`) which are valid; all msr/mrs use `options(nomem,nostack,preserves_flags)` (DAIF/timer regs are not the NZCV condition flags).
flake_fixes_included: Folded in BOTH `include_in_m8` flake fixes (neither left out — both are tagged for M8, both are safe and directly relevant). FLAKE 1 (crates/tb-hal/src/arch/x86_64/user.rs): `const USER_RFLAGS: u64 = 0x202` → `0x2`, clearing IF so M4 ring3 runs MASKED, plus the corrected doc comment. This is exactly aligned with M8: M8 performs the kernel's deliberate FIRST interrupt-enable (sti/daifclr), so M0–M4 MUST run fully masked; the old IF=1 ring3 window was the only asynchrony in M0–M4 and the source of the intermittent TCG-only vector-0 fault. tb-hal-only const + doc; kernel stays unsafe-free; cannot regress the round-trip (`int 0x80` works with IF=0). The diagnosis rated its m4 confidence MEDIUM (the literal vector-0 value was not fully reconciled), but the fix itself is unambiguously safe and is required hardening before M8 enables interrupts, so it is included. FLAKE 2 (scripts/bench-boot.sh): replaced the `coproc QPROC` live-read loop (which ran a per-line `$(date)` command substitution that closes the coprocess fd under bash, dropping every line after the first under the KVM output burst → full=NA every iteration) with a temp-file capture + `grep -qF` poll + post-run drain grep, keeping QEMU's real PID for the watchdog and staying `set -e`-safe. Applied as a separate patch from the FINAL_MARKER bump (both land in bench-boot.sh). Scripts-only; preserves spawn→marker timing. NOTHING was deliberately left out.
